name: Sequence Memory
description: Sequence Memory is a rule-based challenge that asks the player to observe, reason, manipulate state, or sequence actions.
design_notes: Sequence Memory should be designed around a clear player promise, a readable trigger, and a predictable result. For puzzle mechanics, the important design work is deciding when the mechanic is allowed, how much commitment it creates, what feedback confirms success, and what counter-pressure keeps it from solving every situation. Strong tuning makes the mechanic feel intentional even when it is used repeatedly or combined with adjacent systems.
design_purpose: Give the player a readable way to use sequence memory as part of the core loop.
Create meaningful choices around timing, cost, risk, positioning, or long-term planning.
Connect sequence memory to adjacent systems so it supports the larger game design instead of acting as an isolated feature.
player_fantasy: The player feels clever when they infer the rule and manipulate the system correctly.

parameters.name: activation_condition
parameters.description: Rule that determines when the puzzle element changes state.

parameters.name: reset_time
parameters.description: Delay before the puzzle returns to its default state.

parameters.name: feedback_intensity
parameters.description: How strongly the game communicates success, failure, or partial progress.

edge_cases: Sequence Memory is triggered during a scene transition, pause, respawn, or save/load boundary.
Sequence Memory overlaps with another high-priority state such as damage, stun, dialogue, menu focus, or cutscene control.
Sequence Memory runs at extreme frame rates, high latency, very large coordinates, or after long play sessions.
common_bugs: Sequence Memory state does not reset correctly after interruption or reload.
Feedback for Sequence Memory plays even when the mechanic fails or is rejected.
Sequence Memory produces different results between frame rates, platforms, or network clients.
balancing_notes: Make Sequence Memory strong enough to justify attention but limited enough that other mechanics keep their purpose.
Tune costs, cooldowns, ranges, and feedback together; changing only one number can shift the mechanic from optional to dominant.
accessibility_notes: Provide clear visual, audio, or haptic feedback when Sequence Memory becomes available, succeeds, or fails.
Avoid relying on a single sensory cue or extremely narrow timing unless the game offers assists or alternate settings.

implementation_notes.unity: Implement Sequence Memory with engine-agnostic state first, then bind it to MonoBehaviour, CharacterController, Rigidbody, UI Toolkit, or Netcode systems as appropriate.
implementation_notes.godot: Model Sequence Memory as a node or resource-driven state and update deterministic pieces from _physics_process when timing or collision matters.
implementation_notes.unreal: Implement Sequence Memory with Actor Components, CharacterMovement, Gameplay Ability System, UMG, or replicated gameplay state depending on the mechanic scope.
implementation_notes.web: Implement Sequence Memory as data-owned state updated from the main loop; keep rendering, input, and simulation boundaries explicit for Canvas, Phaser, Three.js, or custom JavaScript.
